data.title: Alibaba Launches XuanTie C950 CPU for Agentic AI
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Last week, Alibaba’s DAMO Academy unveiled the XuanTie C950, a 64-bit multi-core CPU designed for agentic AI workloads. It aims to challenge proprietary Western architectures with open-source competition. Alibaba leans heavily into the open-standard RISC-V instruction set architecture.

The company said it pursues architectural sovereignty and shields its operations from volatile U.S. export controls. These controls have severely restricted the flow of advanced Western AI accelerators into China.

Breaking the Western duopoly with Taiwanese manufacturing

To assess the importance of the XuanTie C950 release, it is essential to trace Alibaba’s cadence of microarchitectural refinement over the past several years. Teresa Cervero, RISC-V ambassador and senior research engineer at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC), told EE Times, “The family of XuanTie processors has been evolving at an amazing pace over the last five years, helping bridge the gap with the market leaders.”

The release of the C920 in 2024 optimized the execution pipeline for cloud workloads, while the debut of the C930 in 2025 was heralded as the industry’s first server-grade RISC-V CPU.

The C950, introduced in 2026, goes beyond previous incremental updates. The processor is verified on an advanced 5-nm process node. Sources indicate that TSMC would manufacture it. It operates at a competitive maximum clock frequency of 3.2 GHz.

The C950’s performance benchmarks consistently deliver a SPECint2006 score above 70 points. This single-thread integer performance shows that a design based on an open-source ISA can compete at the highest levels traditionally monopolized by Intel’s x86 and Arm’s proprietary designs.

“​This is an important milestone for the RISC-V community, especially in the HPC domain,” Cervero argued. “The RISC-V HPC Special Group of Interest has been stating for several years that RISC-V is HPC from both the software and hardware stacks, but the lack of high-performance hardware has undermined this fact… until now.”

Independent technical analyses suggest that the C950’s performance profile and execution efficiency place it nearly on par with Apple’s transformative M1 processor. This indicates Alibaba’s ability to quickly close a multi-decade gap in architectural engineering. This momentum is reflected in the broader market: By January 2026, RISC-V had reached roughly 25% of global processor share, suggesting that the entrenched duopoly between x86 and Arm is beginning to loosen.

The open-standard RISC-V ISA allows chip designers to “customize instruction sets and accelerate specific AI workloads with no or low licensing fees,” Alibaba said.

Alibaba versus HiSilicon and SMIC

Assessing the strategic impact of the XuanTie C950 requires comparing it with Huawei Technologies and its chip design division, HiSilicon. Both aim for computational independence, but differ in their approaches. Huawei uses a proprietary Arm architecture in its Kunpeng 920 CPU, built on 7-nm technology. Alibaba relies on the open-standard RISC-V architecture in its C950. Huawei develops the next-generation Kunpeng 930; the C950 currently outperforms the Kunpeng 920 in single-thread integer throughput, processing over twice as many instructions per clock cycle.

The battlefield is equally fierce in the realm of discrete AI accelerators, a sector long dominated by Nvidia. In response to severe U.S. export controls, Alibaba has shifted from consuming Western technology to producing domestic alternatives. Last year, Alibaba launched the Zhenwu 810E (T-Head parallel processing unit) to compete with Nvidia’s H20 and A800 chips. 

This processor reduces reliance on Taiwanese foundries. Industry reports indicate that a domestic Chinese partner, widely believed to be SMIC, is manufacturing the chip using 7-nm technology. Meanwhile, Huawei introduced the Ascend 950PR for inference tasks and aligned its software to CUDA to attract developers.

Alibaba is following a dual strategy spanning short-term procurement and long-term development. Alibaba indeed plans to place massive orders for Huawei’s new Ascend 950PR to meet its immediate cloud infrastructure needs. At the same time, it aggressively funds its own T-Head subsidiary to build the vertically integrated Zhenwu accelerators and the open-source C950 CPUs.

Engineering for agentic AI

The C950 features native, closely coupled AI acceleration. Traditional computing architectures rely on peripheral buses to offload intensive matrix computations to discrete, power-hungry Nvidia GPUs. Alibaba circumvents this by embedding a proprietary tensor processing engine directly into the CPU core complex through the RISC-V Attached Matrix Extension.

This architectural shift mirrors broader industry movements. “As AI is gaining prominence in HPC, XuanTie addresses the convergence/coexistence of traditional HPC and AI by integrating an AI engine into its new CPU,” Cervero explained. “A trend that is aligned with one of the RISC-V standardization activities for matrix extensions.”

Furthermore, by decoupling the matrix from traditional vector computation, the processor enables greater data reuse, the company claimed. This reduces memory bandwidth demand and improves energy efficiency. The tightly integrated hardware is designed to natively support and execute inference for massive foundation models with hundreds of billions of parameters, such as Alibaba’s Qwen3 and DeepSeek V3.

DeepSeek V3 can now run natively on a RISC-V CPU. The model’s Mixture-of-Experts architecture requires hardware capable of sparse computing and dynamic computational graph optimization. The C950 provides the heterogeneous architectural profile needed to leverage these innovations. It significantly reduces the memory access latency that typically plagues GPU-accelerated inference, the company claimed.

This hardware leap coincides with the viral explosion of agentic AI in early 2026, exemplified by open-source frameworks such as OpenClaw that can autonomously operate computers and manage enterprise workflows. In direct response to this market demand, Alibaba launched “Wukong,” an AI-native enterprise platform integrated into its DingTalk collaboration suite, and “Accio Work,” an international agent designed to automate complex, cross-border business operations. 

Deploying large-scale autonomous agent systems requires low-latency, cost-efficient inference hardware. Alibaba claimed its C950 provides the architectural profile required to operate these platforms profitably, insulating the company from the severe margin compression that comes with relying on expensive Nvidia or Huawei arrays for high-frequency, logic-intensive tasks.

Chinese state support and T-Head potential IPO

Alibaba’s corporate efforts align with state-backed initiatives for technological independence. The DAMO Academy has forged strategic agreements with the Chinese Academy of Sciences to jointly develop the next-generation Xiangshan core. They pool vast resources to tackle complex engineering challenges, such as advanced on-chip interconnects. By collaborating with state academic institutions, Alibaba accelerates and commercializes foundational RISC-V research through its supply chain.

The commercialization of this ecosystem is spearheaded by Alibaba’s semiconductor subsidiary, T-Head, which has achieved cumulative global deliveries of over 470,000 AI chips. Annualized revenue has rapidly approached 10 billion yuan (~$1.45 billion) over the past two years. This growth trajectory has prompted persistent, credible market speculation regarding a potential independent initial public offering for T-Head. 

Furthermore, Alibaba CEO Eddie Wu has explicitly outlined a long-term strategic vision to transition Alibaba into a full-stack AI technology provider, encompassing everything from foundational silicon to upper-layer cloud infrastructure. A successful spinoff and public offering of T-Head would not only unlock massive shareholder value but also inject billions of dollars of capital directly into expanding the Chinese RISC-V ecosystem.

data.title: Rohm Joins Toshiba and Mitsubishi to Create a Power Chip Titan
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The news about Rohm Semiconductor joining hands with Toshiba and Mitsubishi Electric to create a trifecta power semiconductor unit marks Japan Inc.’s strategic quest for relevance in a market increasingly defined by scale and global competitiveness. That kicks off with Toshiba Electronic Devices & Storage Corp. (TDSC), a subsidiary of Toshiba, signing a memorandum of understanding to begin discussions with Mitsubishi and Rohm.

This new company will further bolster Japan’s global presence following memory stalwart Kioxia and could become the world’s second largest power chip supplier after Germany’s Infineon Technologies. The three Japanese companies in this tie-up collectively hold 11% of the global power semiconductor market, compared to Infineon’s 24%.

Power semiconductors are a crucial segment amid high-growth markets such as EVs, renewable energy, data centers, and industrial automation. And this combo has the recipe to target these vital power electronics markets.

Rohm develops and manufactures a very large product range, including low-power MCUs, power management ICs, silicon carbide (SiC) diodes, MOSFETs and modules, power transistors and diodes, and LEDs. Its SiC device portfolio is especially crucial for EV inverters, charging systems, renewable energy, and industrial drives.

Mitsubishi Electric’s power device unit also sells SiC MOSFETs alongside other power semiconductors and driver ICs. Next, Toshiba TDSC supplies MCUs, power management ICs, and other power semiconductors.

Meanwhile, the Japanese components supplier Denso also has skin in the game. The company entered a strategic partnership with Rohm and later acquired a 5% stake in the Kyoto-based chipmaker.

The Denso twist

The plot thickens with Denso’s recent attempt to acquire Rohm for an estimated $8.3 billion to bolster Toyota’s power electronics supply chain. Denso, Toyota’s key components supplier, approached the Kyoto-based chipmaker with an acquisition proposal after the two companies signed a strategic partnership to focus on chips for EVs.

One of the stark lessons from the recent Nexperia saga and the resulting troubles of Honda and Nissan is that a heavy reliance on China for semiconductors carries risks. Automakers such as Honda and Nissan were shaken after China moved to disrupt the supply of Nexperia chips manufactured at local plants.

While Rohm created a special committee to consider Denso’s offer, news of a three-way tie-up to create a power semiconductor powerhouse has further complicated this affair with Denso. If this tie-up leads to the creation of a power semiconductor unit, it’d be interesting to see Denso’s stake in this new chip firm.

It’s worth noting that, according to TechInsights data, Rohm held 2.8% of the global power semiconductor market in 2025, while Mitsubishi Electric and Toshiba accounted for 3% and 1.7%, respectively. And Denso’s share of the power semiconductor market was merely 1%.

Cost competitiveness through integration

In retrospect, it’s a pretty simple call for Mitsubishi Electric to spin off its semiconductor business. The company, which specializes in industrial-based power semiconductors, has been considering various options to make its semiconductor business more competitive. Mitsubishi Electric is a leading manufacturer of industrial and electronic equipment.

Toshiba, after spinning off its memory business as Kioxia, seems to be in a similar quandary regarding scale, cost competitiveness, and new technology development. In fact, in December 2023, Toshiba’s TDSC unit joined hands with Rohm to submit to the Japanese government a production plan to a secure stable supply of semiconductors domestically.

Finally, Rohm has long been exploring the possibility of leveraging its strengths in power semiconductors and thus making its business more competitive globally in terms of scale and technology. This tie-up with Mitsubishi Electric and Toshiba TDSC offers the venue it’s long sought to bolster its standing in the semiconductor industry, domestically and globally.

It will also boost Japan Inc., which is increasingly worried about its shrinking footprint in the semiconductor industry. This consolidation will also address the concerns regarding Japan’s fragmented semiconductor market.

While it’s not a done deal yet, the rationale behind this initiative for the creation of a power semiconductor specialist looks strong. Meanwhile, another player on the outset, Toyota’s components supplier Denso, will be keenly watching the developments and its own relevance in this Japanese affair.

data.title: U.S. Startup Emergence AI Opens Research Hub in Bengaluru
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Emergence AI, a New York-based agentic AI company founded by three Indian-origin researchers, has launched Emergence India Labs (EIL) in Bengaluru, India, positioning it as a core research hub rather than a satellite outpost. Backed by tens of millions of dollars in initial funding, EIL expects to expand to around 500 researchers over the next three to four years. Its ambition is to build autonomous AI systems for mission-critical applications.

The startup attempts to solve a problem that has limited enterprise adoption of AI: reliability. While large language models (LLMs) and neural systems have advanced rapidly, their probabilistic nature makes them difficult to deploy in environments where consistency is required. Emergence AI builds autonomous AI agents that combine probabilistic models such as LLMs with a deterministic, formally verified control layer, where natural language instructions are converted into mathematical lemmas and validated using theorem provers to ensure reliable, mission-critical execution.

“Emergence India Labs is about combining neural networks and symbolic logic,” said Satya Nitta, co-founder and CEO of Emergence AI. “It is a combination of the past and the future of artificial intelligence, where these major fields come together to build autonomous systems.”

Founded in 2018 by former IBM researchers Satya Nitta, CEO, Ravi Kokk, CTO, and Sharad C. Sundararajan, CIO, the startup’s central innovation lies in what it calls a formally verified control architecture. Instead of relying solely on probabilistic models, Emergence AI introduces deterministic layers that enforce mathematical guarantees.

“Neural networks and large language models are powerful but probabilistic,” Nitta said. “For mission-critical applications, you cannot rely only on probabilistic systems.”

He illustrated the limitation with a simple example: Combining two models that are each 95% accurate does not improve reliability. “In the simplest case, combining probabilities results in around 0.95 multiplied by 0.95, which is about 90.25%,” he said, adding that real-world performance can drop further.

Located at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) Bangalore, EIL is intended to serve as a nucleus for a broader ecosystem involving academia, startups, and industry. It will collaborate with IISc through joint research, hackathons, and training programs.

“What India historically has not had…is places where an enormous pool of deep talent can go to and do world-class, world-leading research,” Nitta said. “I spent two decades at IBM Research and have seen how hub-and-spoke research models operate. The main center is where the agenda is set and where most breakthroughs occur. So, we chose to place the core lab in Bengaluru rather than New York.”

Product architecture and operation

Emergence’s product consists of autonomous agents deployed within enterprise environments. These agents integrate directly into systems rather than operating as external application programming interfaces. “These agents are self-improving,” Nitta said.

The architecture follows a loop-based system involving planning, execution, verification, and memory. Natural language inputs are first converted into mathematical lemmas, using Lean, a programming language designed to convert natural language into mathematical form. This is followed by planning and execution. A verification layer checks outputs against constraints and ground truth using theorem provers, sending the system back for reprocessing if inconsistencies are detected. This validation process, known as formal verification, ensures deterministic outputs.

“For example, businesses operate with rules,” Nitta said. “An airline may have a rule that a crew cannot operate for more than 10 hours at a time. If this is interpreted by a probabilistic model, it may not always be applied correctly. We convert such rules into mathematical statements, which are verified with certainty.”

Nitta continued, “If the verifier is not confident, it asks the system to try again. This continues until the correct reasoning is achieved.”

The system also incorporates memory for reuse and self-improvement. “If a similar task has been solved before, the system can reuse that knowledge,” he said.

Unlike traditional software, the startup’s AI agents dynamically generate models and code. “They can create machine learning models dynamically,” he said, adding that they function like “data scientists or data engineers with advanced expertise.”

Business model and infrastructure requirements

Although the systems rely on GPUs for inference, Emergence AI does not operate on the same compute scale as LLM developers. “We are not training large language models,” Nitta said. “That is where the highest compute demand lies. We operate in a different part of the artificial intelligence stack.”

He added that compute requirements can decrease over time as workloads stabilize and smaller models replace larger ones. The startup positions itself as a layer above foundational AI models rather than a competitor.

“We are building on top of them,” Nitta said. “We are not competing at the large language model layer. As these models improve, our systems also improve.”

The company operates in what it calls the “action space,” where AI systems execute tasks rather than just generate outputs. This space, he argues, is too broad for any single player to dominate. “The range of possible tasks is very large, so there is space for many players,” he said.

The business model is based on licensing. “Typically, customers license the agents and pay a licensing fee,” he said. “If there is customization, there is an additional fee.”

The startup is targeting large enterprises dealing with mission-critical operations across sectors such as semiconductors, oil and gas, biotechnology, airlines, and consumer products. “The focus is less on the sector and more on the type of problem,” said Nitta, describing environments characterized by high data volume, velocity, variety, and veracity.

Use cases closest to production are in semiconductor manufacturing, particularly yield analysis and new product ramp-up. “We are currently in advanced pilot stages and expect to scale these deployments soon,” he added.

To date, Emergence AI has raised about $97.2 million in funding.

Moving past the headwinds

Despite its ambitions, the company acknowledges technical limitations. Not all tasks can be formally verified. “We expect tasks to fall into three categories: Those that can be formally verified, those that cannot, and those that can be partially verified,” Nitta said.

This creates inherent constraints on where the technology can be applied. Additionally, the lack of a comprehensive library of mathematical lemmas means new deployments often require fresh development. “There is no comprehensive library of lemmas for all scenarios yet,” he noted.

Market readiness is another challenge. Enterprise hesitation toward AI remains high due to concerns around reliability. “Many artificial intelligence deployments have not scaled because organizations do not fully trust these systems,” he said.

Despite the constraints, the startup remains optimistic about its growth across geographies, with the U.S. currently its primary market and early traction in the Middle East. India is expected to become a significant contributor over time.

The broader opportunity lies in sectors such as semiconductor manufacturing, solar energy, and robotics, where automation and autonomous decision-making are becoming essential.

Looking ahead, Nitta sees a mixed competitive landscape. “Some capabilities will be absorbed by large model providers and cloud platforms,” he said. “At the same time, there is room for new companies focused on domain-specific and mission-critical systems.”

He added that AI remains a “customization-driven field,” which allows multiple players to coexist.

The launch of EIL reflects a wider transition in AI, from model development to system-level reliability and deployment. “The debate is no longer only about training the largest language models, but about developing the autonomous systems layer,” the startup stated in its announcement.

Emergence is betting that formal verification and deterministic control will define the next phase of AI adoption, especially in environments where failure is not an option.

data.title: Europe’s Semiconductor Strategy Meets Market Reality
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Europe’s semiconductor strategy has largely been framed as a geopolitical balancing act. Policymakers in Brussels speak of “de-risking, not decoupling,” aiming to reduce strategic dependencies without dismantling the global openness that built the modern chip industry.

But a more consequential stress test is emerging—one driven less by export controls and more by market economics.

For Antonia Hmaidi, analyst at the Mercator Institute for China Studies, the semiconductor sector has already crossed into a new strategic era. “Semiconductors have become central to global strategic competition,” she told EE Times. The shift is not merely rhetorical: Chips now sit squarely at the intersection of economic security and national resilience.

That raises a harder question for Europe. Building resilience is one thing; proving the continent’s semiconductor push can remain commercially durable in a rapidly shifting global market is another.

Europe’s careful middle path

Brussels has been deliberate in how it positions its semiconductor strategy. Unlike Washington’s more confrontational posture toward Beijing, the European Union continues to emphasize calibration. The language of de-risking is meant to signal selectivity—tightening scrutiny in sensitive areas while preserving broader economic engagement.

Hmaidi was explicit about the distinction. “De-risking is not the same as decoupling,” she said, noting that European policymakers are trying to reduce the most critical vulnerabilities while keeping channels of interdependence open.

Whether that middle path holds will depend in part on how Europe is perceived in Beijing. Chinese policymakers are closely watching how Brussels responds to Washington’s technology posture.

“From the Chinese perspective, the key question is whether Europe will ultimately align more closely with Washington,” Hmaidi said, adding that for now, the answer remains deliberately ambiguous.

From the industry side, executives say the shift has already moved from theory to operating reality.

“There’s a fundamental change in the way the three major regions interact compared to a few years ago,” Jens Drews, director of government affairs for EMEA at GlobalFoundries, told EE Times. “The change is not cyclical; it’s structural.”

Pandemic disruptions, geopolitical shifts, and tightening export controls have combined to force a broad reassessment of risk across the industry. In particular, U.S. technology restrictions have narrowed Europe’s strategic room for maneuver, even if they have not fully dictated European policy.

Drews put it bluntly: U.S. measures have “forced Europe to confront a few tough questions” about technology dependencies and supply chain exposure. Even so, Europe is not simply mirroring Washington. Coordination around advanced-node safeguards exists, but it remains targeted rather than comprehensive.

Europe’s most visible shift has been its renewed embrace of industrial policy. Through the EU Chips Act and parallel national programs, policymakers are attempting to rebuild domestic manufacturing capacity and reduce external dependencies.

Hmaidi sees this as part of a broader global trend. Governments that once treated semiconductors primarily as commercial infrastructure now increasingly view them as strategic assets. But she also warns against overestimating how far regionalization can realistically go. “There are clear limits to what regionalization can achieve in such a deeply globalized industry,” she said.

Drews noted that the semiconductor value chain remains one of the most globally distributed in advanced manufacturing. “Our industry is probably more globalized than anything else,” he said. Attempting full end-to-end autonomy within one region, he added, “is not manageable.” His preferred framing—“Made with Europe. Not made in Europe alone.”—captures the pragmatic middle ground that many manufacturers are advocating for.

Hmaidi said that while European debate has focused heavily on security and technological sovereignty, it should not ignore a quieter but potentially more immediate threat: China’s rapid expansion of mature-node manufacturing capacity. “We are already seeing signs of potential overcapacity in some mature-node segments,” she said. “If Chinese producers significantly increase output, European companies could face real pricing pressure.”

The distinction between geopolitical and economic pressure is becoming clearer: Subsidizing new fabs is a political decision; sustaining them through downcycles is an economic one.

Geopolitics enters the fab equation

“Geopolitical factors have moved up on the scale of things that need to be considered,” Drews said. Because fabs are multibillion-euro assets with lifetimes measured in decades, companies are placing increasing weight on regulatory stability and geographic diversification. What once looked like redundancy now looks like resilience.

GlobalFoundries’ multi-region footprint, historically viewed as inefficient, has become a competitive advantage, Drews said, allowing customers to dual-source across continents and hedge against tariffs or export disruptions.

Importantly, this does not signal de-globalization. The semiconductor industry is not becoming local; it is becoming strategically distributed.

Perhaps the most underappreciated vulnerability in Europe’s semiconductor strategy lies on the demand side. So far, policy attention has focused overwhelmingly on boosting supply through subsidies and capacity targets. But sustaining high-utilization fabs ultimately requires strong and predictable local demand.

Drews argues that this is where Europe may need to sharpen its focus. “In Europe, we have been very good at addressing supply,” he said. “I think Europe also needs to take care of demand for capacities in Europe.”

This concern intersects directly with Hmaidi’s warning about mature-node expansion. If global supply grows faster than demand, utilization—and margins—could come under pressure, particularly in more commoditized segments.

A future defined by managed tension

Looking ahead, Hmaidi does not expect a clean break in EU–China semiconductor ties. The more likely trajectory is continued selective interdependence combined with targeted controls. “We should expect managed tension rather than full decoupling,” she said.

For Europe, the next phase of its semiconductor strategy may be determined less by diplomatic positioning than by economic performance. Three critical questions are coming into focus:

  • Can Europe generate enough demand to sustain new capacity? Europe’s policy focus to date has leaned heavily toward supply creation: subsidizing fabs, attracting foundry investment, and expanding local manufacturing footprints. But fabs do not run on policy ambition alone; they run on steady wafer demand. Without a stronger pull from Europe’s automotive, industrial, and emerging AI hardware ecosystems, new capacity risks operate below optimal utilization. That would not only pressure margins but could also weaken the political case for continued public support. The next phase of the EU Chips Act may therefore hinge less on how many fabs Europe can build and more on whether it can cultivate durable, local demand to keep them full.
  • Can its fabs remain competitive if mature-node pricing weakens? China’s rapid expansion in mature-node production introduces a classic semiconductor risk: oversupply. If pricing pressure intensifies in 28 nm, 40 nm, and other legacy nodes—segments where Europe has meaningful exposure—the economics of new or expanded European capacity could tighten quickly. Subsidies can help offset upfront capital costs, but they do not guarantee long-term competitiveness in a downcycle. The real test will be whether European fabs can differentiate on specialty processes, automotive reliability, or supply chain assurance strongly enough to defend margins if global pricing softens. In a commoditizing segment, operational efficiency and customer stickiness may matter more than geopolitical positioning.
  • Can Brussels maintain openness while tightening strategic controls? Europe’s semiconductor strategy rests on a delicate dual objective: Reduce critical dependencies while remaining an attractive and globally connected market. That balance becomes harder to maintain as investment screening expands, export controls evolve, and pressure for “local-for-local” policies grows worldwide. Move too slowly, and Europe risks leaving strategic vulnerabilities exposed. Move too aggressively, and it could deter the very foreign investment and ecosystem partnerships that underpin its semiconductor ambitions. The durability of Europe’s middle path—de-risking without drifting into de facto decoupling—will be tested not in policy speeches but in capital allocation decisions by global chipmakers.

For Europe’s chip ambitions, the real stress test is no longer political resolve but sustained, profitable demand.

data.title: Why AI in Semiconductor Inspection Is Becoming a Two-Layer Game
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping semiconductor defect detection, but not along a single trajectory. Instead, the industry is beginning to coalesce around two distinct, and potentially complementary, architectural approaches. One embeds AI tightly within advanced inspection hardware to extract new physical insight from wafers; the other sits above the manufacturing data stack, correlating signals across tools to flag emerging yield risks.

Exclusive interviews with Kevin Berghoff, CEO of QuantumDiamonds, and Dieter Rathei, CEO of DR Yield, illustrate how these two schools of thought are taking shape as fabs struggle to manage both the physics complexity of advanced nodes and the data deluge of modern production lines.

Together, they point toward a future in which defect detection becomes a multi-layer intelligence problem rather than a single-tool capability.

Physics-informed AI at the sensor level

QuantumDiamonds represents the first camp: companies building AI deeply coupled to proprietary sensing hardware. The firm’s quantum diamond microscope generates magnetic field maps of ICs, which its software then converts into current-flow information inside the chip.

“We built completely in-house the software that classifies defects,” Berghoff said. The company’s core challenge is reconstructing current distributions from magnetic field measurements—an inverse problem that requires physics-informed machine learning rather than purely statistical analysis.

The workflow begins with customer layout data. QuantumDiamonds simulates expected current flow for a known-good die, then compares that baseline with measured magnetic field data from the actual device. AI models classify anomalies that may indicate shorts, opens, or material defects.

Because the approach is rooted in a new sensing modality, the AI is tightly bound to the hardware. “The core of what we do is interpreting those magnetic fields to detect defects,” Berghoff said.

This deep coupling is both a strength and a constraint. On the positive side, the system can surface failure mechanisms that may be invisible to conventional inspection. Berghoff said the company has demonstrated the ability to find defects that would otherwise remain hidden, though he declined to quantify yield improvements.

However, the current generation of systems is optimized for sample-based analysis rather than full inline inspection. Wafers are pulled from the line and analyzed offline, with full inline capability still several years away. Even so, Berghoff sees the long-term trajectory clearly. The “golden end state,” he said, is to fuse multiple physical data sources—optical, X-ray, and magnetic—into a unified defect classification framework.

That vision hints at the second major AI architecture now gaining traction.

A fab-wide data intelligence layer

DR Yield represents a very different starting point. Rather than building new sensing hardware, the company focuses on aggregating and analyzing the vast quantities of data already generated across semiconductor fabs.

Founded more than two decades ago, the company’s YieldWatchDog platform was designed to automatically scan manufacturing data and flag anomalies that engineers might miss. “The idea was to integrate all data from different sources,” Rathei said. “There are so many data in the industry, and they are siloed into different data warehouses.”

The platform ingests electrical test results, inline metrology, defect inspection data, and equipment signals—essentially any numerical dataset available in the fab. From there, statistical and AI models look for early warning signals for yield excursions.

Unlike QuantumDiamonds’ physics-heavy approach, DR Yield’s models are primarily statistical, though adapted specifically for semiconductor manufacturing geometries and workflows. “We provide a yield analytics solution,” Rathei said. “As long as the data are numerical, we can look for anomalies in trends or spatial patterns on wafers.”

One of the system’s key strengths is multivariate correlation. Rathei described cases in which individual parameters remained within control limits, but their relationship broke down—an early indicator of tool degradation.

“In one study, pressure and flow inside a tool were both within control,” he said. “However, the correlation between those two broke down because a valve was getting leaky. You didn’t see that if you looked at each parameter separately.”

Such cross-signal intelligence is increasingly important as fabs deploy dozens of specialized inspection tools, each generating its own data stream.

Different layers, not direct competitors

At first glance, the two approaches might appear to compete. In practice, they operate at different layers of the manufacturing intelligence stack. QuantumDiamonds is focused on extracting new physical insight from individual devices; DR Yield is focused on connecting signals across the fab to detect systemic risk.

Rathei acknowledged the distinction directly when asked about defect classification: “The defect classification itself is not something that we try to achieve with YieldWatchDog.” Instead, the company concentrates on high-volume data ingestion, fast access, and anomaly detection across datasets. That positioning makes the technologies more complementary than competitive.

Hardware vendors typically provide analytics specific to their own tools, Rathei noted. DR Yield’s role is to “put this layer on top to connect everything.”

Another key difference lies in deployment timing. QuantumDiamonds today operates primarily in sample-based workflows, with inline capability still under development; DR Yield positions its software as “near-real-time,” with data typically available to engineers within seconds or minutes of generation. In modern fabs, both timescales matter.

Deep physical inspection can uncover subtle failure mechanisms that statistical monitoring might miss. Conversely, fab-wide analytics can surface systemic drift long before individual devices fail catastrophically. The result is an emerging multi-timescale intelligence model.

Both companies also reflect the broader evolution of AI in semiconductor manufacturing. QuantumDiamonds uses physics-informed ML to solve inverse electromagnetic problems; DR Yield employs a mix of statistical models, supervised learning, and unsupervised clustering for wafer pattern analysis.

More recently, DR Yield has begun integrating large language models into its platform. The company’s Yield AIssistant allows engineers to query wafer data conversationally and generate automated reports. “We developed an interface that allows people to analyze the data and ask a language model questions about what they see,” Rathei said.

Even here, however, the company emphasizes human oversight, a theme that resonates across the industry. “It’s an assistant,” Rathei stressed, not an autonomous decision-maker.

Toward a hybrid future

If the two approaches start from different philosophies, both executives ultimately converge on a similar long-term view: Fabs will likely need multiple layers of AI working together. Berghoff’s vision of fusing magnetic, optical, and X-ray data points toward increasingly rich physical inspection stacks. Rathei argues that fab-level correlation will remain essential as process complexity grows.

When asked how he would architect a greenfield fab, Rathei was unequivocal: “I would definitely do both.” At the tool level, specialized AI is needed for signal extraction and physics interpretation. At the factory level, a broader intelligence layer is required to connect the dots across processes and tools. That dual-layer model may ultimately define the next phase of semiconductor manufacturing analytics.

The backdrop to this evolution is the sheer scale of data that modern fabs must manage. As inspection sensitivity improves and process steps multiply, engineers are increasingly overwhelmed by information. Rathei recalled that many major yield excursions historically showed early warning signs in the data—signals that simply went unnoticed because no one had time to sift through the noise. “There are just not enough people in any given factory to deal with all the data,” he said.

At the same time, new sensing modalities, such as quantum diamond microscopy, are expanding the universe of measurable physical effects inside chips. Together, these trends are pushing defect detection toward a layered AI architecture in which no single tool or model has full visibility.

Defect detection is evolving from a point-tool capability into a systems-level intelligence challenge. Physics-aware inspection will continue to push the boundaries of what can be measured on individual devices. Data-centric analytics will increasingly determine how quickly fabs can respond to subtle process drift. The winners in advanced manufacturing will likely be those who can integrate both.

As AI moves deeper into the fab, the question is no longer whether to deploy machine intelligence in defect detection; it’s where—and at how many layers.

data.title: Arm Launches First Silicon CPU, Targets Data Center Agentic AI Workloads
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Arm finally made it official this week: the launch of its first production ready silicon, the Arm AGI CPU, co-developed in conjunction with Meta and targeting agentic AI workloads in the data center. It was dubbed by some as Arm 2.0, a new era for Arm, and Arm CEO Rene Haas highlighted the company’s shift into selling silicon for the first time, as opposed to just licensing IP.

Arm CEO Rene Haas reveals Arm’s first ever production silicon chip, the AGI CPU (Image: Nitin Dahad)

This would be a new thread to Arm’s business model. The company would continue licensing its processor IP to customers looking to do that, or reference platforms in the form of compute subsystems (CSS). At the same time, the Arm AGI CPU would be offered to data center customers needing a production-ready, application-specific CPU for growing agentic AI workloads in the data center.

The news was announced at an event involving Arm, Meta, OpenAI, and several launch partners and ecosystem partners at the Festival Pavilion in San Francisco, California, on Tuesday, with views of the Golden Gate Bridge and Alcatraz.

Arm said the new CPU would form the foundation for agentic data centers, with silicon providing the ecosystem with greater flexibility in how they build and deploy Arm-based infrastructure—whether licensing Arm IP, adopting Arm CSS, or deploying Arm-designed silicon.

The new Arm AGI CPU features up to 136 Arm Neoverse V3 cores and 96 lanes PCIe Gen6, and is available this year in a TSMC 3nm process. (Source: Arm)

According to Arm’s launch information, the CPU features:

  • Up to 136 Arm Neoverse V3 cores per CPU, delivering leading performance per core, SoC, blade and rack, with 6GB/s memory bandwidth per core at sub-100ns latency.
  • 300-W TDP with a dedicated core per program thread enabling deterministic performance under sustained load, eliminating throttling and idle threads.
  • Support for high-density 1U server chassis that supports air-cooled deployments with up to 8,160 cores per rack, and liquid-cooled systems delivering 45,000+ cores per rack.

Not competing with data center customers

Contrary to many commentators concerns that Arm would simply be competing with its own customers by launching its own silicon, the event and show floor showcasing server racks and demos seemed to exude visible excitement about the development. EE Times spoke to many people and the words used were almost unanimous: it provides them with optionality for data centers.

The target is data centers needing many CPU cores, not the low power microcontroller market. So, Arm is more likely to go head-on against x86 architectures in the data center, or even Nvidia Vera CPUs in some cases.

According to Counterpoint Research, AI hyperscalers are projected to ship more than 10 million AI ASICs in 2028. Currently, most of these ASICs are “attached” to x86 CPUs. The co-founder of Counterpoint, Neil Shah, said in a blog post, “Arm’s AGI CPU provides a purpose-built, native alternative that eliminates the inefficiencies of mixing architectures, offering a seamless Arm-on-Arm environment for AI accelerators.”

In fact, Shah said, “The pressure is on the x86 camp to protect its market share and position. It remains to be seen how Intel and AMD react, whether with pricing or a better architecture approach or software, areas they planned to address with their x86 Ecosystem Advisory Group formed 18 months ago.”

On the flip side, customers’ own design teams could stick to building in house, and Shah comments, “While AI hyperscalers look to diversify with heterogeneous architecture, with some already licensing Arm Neoverse cores, those with strong internal teams may be less inclined to buy the Arm AGI CPU off-the-shelf, which could limit some TAM [total addressable market for Arm].”  

In the media Q&A session, Mohamed Awad, executive VP for the cloud AI business unit at Arm, responded to a question about how customers developing their own CPUs would see the new chip, by highlighting the optionality it gives customers and that they were free to choose from Arm’s complete portfolio.

 “We can walk into any of these customers and say, here’s a portfolio of products. We can give you IP, we can give you CSS, and we can give you the AGI CPU,” Awad said. “In some cases, they may just want IP, in others they may want CSS, and in others they may want a finished piece of silicon in the form of the AGI CPU. And guess what? They get software leverage across all those products. So, what it really gives them at the end of the day is optionality.”

The revenue opportunity: 10× growth in gross profits

The move from IP to an “application specific general-purpose compute” product (as analyst Leonard Lee of neXt Curve suggested it should be called) potentially brings Arm into a completely new league financially. Shah of Counterpoint points this out, highlighting that traditionally Arm could earn a few dollars in royalties per server chip. But by selling the full AGI CPU, the ASP shifts to hundreds or thousands of dollars per unit, representing a 10×-50× increase in revenue per socket.

“Arm is estimating $500 per chip in gross profits versus $50 currently through selling IP or $100 per chip through CSS,” Shah said.

Arm expects FYE28 to be the first year for meaningful Arm AGI CPU revenue and its exponential growth from there, according to Shah. By 2031, Arm expects $10 billion in IP/CSS revenue and $15 billion in AGI CPU sales, targeting a $25-billion annual revenue that year. 

Customers and the roadmap for more CPUs

Arm stated that Meta is its lead partner and customer, co-developing the Arm AGI CPU to optimize gigawatt-scale infrastructure for its Meta family of apps and to work alongside Meta’s own custom MTIA accelerators. Other launch partners include Cerebras, Cloudflare, F5, OpenAI, Positron, Rebellions, SAP, and SK Telecom. Each of these are working with Arm on the deployment of the Arm AGI CPU to accelerate AI-driven services across cloud, networking, and enterprise environments. Arm also said commercial systems are now available for order from ASRockRack, Lenovo, and Supermicro.

The Arm AGI CPU is just the first of Arm’s silicon CPUs. Arm’s CEO presented a roadmap on stage, indicating that follow-on products are committed, continuing in parallel with the Arm Neoverse CSS product roadmap so that all Arm data center customers move forward together on platform architecture and software compatibility.

Arm’s CPU roadmap presented by the company’s CEO, Rene Haas. (Image source: Nitin Dahad)

Since the new CPU is hugely complex, design tools are key to getting something like this to market. So, it’s not surprising that Synopsys also announced this week that it supported the design of Arm’s CPU with solutions across its full-stack design portfolio including EDA, interface IP, and hardware-assisted verification.

In a separate announcement tied to Arm’s launch, Meta said, “Through our collaboration with Arm, we’ll co-develop multiple generations of cutting-edge CPUs built to enable massive compute power in limited space, supporting the AI-optimized data centers and large gigawatt-scale AI deployments that are central to our AI innovations.”

The Arm AGI CPU has been developed with Meta to optimize infrastructure for Meta’s family of apps and work alongside its custom MTIA silicon.

Watch the interviews

EE Times spoke to a number of people to get a snapshot of the mood at the Arm Everywhere launch event. You can watch them below.

data.title: Ex-Tenstorrent Execs Start Cloud Provider, AI Lab in Japan
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI& (“AI And”), a new vertically-integrated Japanese AI company from former Tenstorrent and Lenovo executives David Bennett and Shimpei Hara, has raised $50 million in seed funding and $2 billion in infrastructure capital.

AI& wants to tackle every part of the AI stack from the data center upwards, including building out Japanese data center infrastructure as well as developing orchestration/cluster management software for heterogeneous data centers, plus AI models, agents, and applications.  

At GTC 2026 last week, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang postulated that all employees of all companies will be running 100 AI agents each in the near future. If scaled to Japan’s population, with each agent using one million tokens per day, total power demand would reach a terawatt, Shimpei Hara, AI& president and cofounder, told EE Times.

“If this is where we’re heading, who is going to be the server of AI in Japan?” he said. “And how can you serve Japan without breaking the bank and without needing a terawatt of power, because that’s definitely not the direction our government is heading towards.”

AI adoption at such scale will require a vertically-integrated approach to be economical and efficient, Hara said. This will mean AI& can optimize at every layer of the stack to improve the speed and quality of tokens while enabling some flexibility in pricing.

“Others are trying to carve out a technical specialty within their specific domain and make a business out of it, so there’s margin stacking, to some extent…which results in a highly inflated end token cost and AI deployment cost,” he said. “We believe that having ownership across the stack and optimizing through that is actually going to help us reduce the cost of serving overall, specific to the Japan market.”

Japanese market

The company will focus on Japan, with a view of a global rollout further down the line, AI& CEO and cofounder David Bennett told EE Times.

“[The Japanese market] is driven by AWS and the big hyperscalers,” Bennett said. “We see a real opportunity because there are complexity and price issues dealing with the big guys. Whether there are security or privacy reasons, or other workload-specific things, there’s certainly a need for players like us.”

The real problem is how to get enterprises up and running with AI, he said, since they need to engage higher up the stack than the hardware level. In this case, great technology isn’t enough—it has to be easy to use.

There’s a big demand for data sovereignty, Bennett added.

“People want to own their data, they want open models, they also want to know that if they’re putting their data into [online AI chatbots] where that data is going,” he said. “We’ll be able to say it’s staying in our data centers in Japan.”

AI& plans to prove its concept in Japan before looking at Southeast Asia, Europe, and beyond.

Heterogeneous hardware

The infrastructure and staff of Japanese AI cloud provider Unsung Fields have been folded into AI& (Unsung Fields has ceased its operations). This means AI& already has two Japanese data centers up and running, offering more than 1,000 GPUs plus a cluster of Tenstorrent hardware.

The heterogeneity of its data centers is a key part of the company’s strategy, Bennett said.

“Being able to send the right workload to the right hardware, and frankly, the right kind of token to the right user—we really believe in the heterogeneous aspect,” he said. “Whether it’s disaggregation strategies or not having to be limited in your supply chain to a single vendor, [heterogeneity] is obviously important.”

The company already has a significant amount of Nvidia GPUs and is planning to work with AMD hardware, too, Hara said. The company expects to open another data center in Japan within a month to help serve its 80 existing customers.

“Our first heterogeneous experiments are around how to get efficient token throughput gains by disaggregating across AMD and Nvidia,” he said. “We think that’s a really interesting thing to continue to push, [because] even a 1.5 to 2× in token throughput is [worth it].”

As well as its GPU clusters, AI& is already generating revenues from Unsung Fields’ Tenstorrent hardware.

“AI& has probably the largest installation of Tenstorrent hardware anywhere, and we’re really looking forward to what they’re doing with Blackhole,” Bennett said.

Acknowledging that heterogeneity can add complexity to the stack, the company will start simple, Bennett said.  

“We’re not creating complexity in our tech stack for the fun of it,” he said. “It’s quite simple today, we can route the right model to the right place, that’s super easy. We’re going to be very selective in how we use our heterogeneity and how complex we get. If there’s really no value, we’re not going to do it.”

Japanese AI lab

AI& also plans to become Japan’s premier AI lab.

The big AI labs will continue to build foundation models, but there’s also space for development of more application-specific models, he said.

“If you can put the right amount of effort into it, the right amount of data in pre-training, post-training and RL [reinforcement learning], and then you build the right harness, you can provide some workloads, experiences and use cases that outperform the foundation models on very specific tasks,” he said. “In markets that are under-served because they’re not English-based, or they have different needs, they can be better served when you have a team that’s designing and building models, including foundation level models that cater to that market.”

AI& also plans to become an incubator for Japanese AI startups, enabled by being vertically integrated, Bennett said.

“There’s a lot of AI startups in Japan that have great ideas and need a ton of compute,” he said. “I think it’s a perfect match for us to incubate these startups, and we can do some really interesting things around…business models where we can both benefit.”

Ultimately, there are a lot of focused AI researchers and developers in Japan who want to do something in Japan, for Japan, and they need somewhere to do that, Bennett said.

“Japan has a lot of talent and they need a place to go,” Bennett said. “We want to be that place to go.”

Cost-Efficient AI at Scale is a Software Problem

data.title: Renaissance Fusion Targets Cost-Competitive Fusion
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Oil and natural gas supplies have become less certain over the last few weeks, and this has increased interest in alternative sources of energy, such as fusion. As you might recall, fusion machines maintain a hot plasma within a donut-shaped structure. Nuclear fusion differs from nuclear fission, which generates electricity with uranium fuel. Fission isn’t popular due to meltdown risk, nuclear waste, proliferation risk, and cost. Fusion doesn’t face these issues but is still in development.

For fusion to be useful, it must produce electricity at a cost below that of fossil fuels. This is theoretically referred to as “economic fusion,” and it would entail low-cost continuous operation. Renaissance Fusion, a company based in Grenoble, France, is leading the way to produce such a machine.

Founded by Francisco Volpe in 2020, Renaissance Fusion employs over 100 people and has raised over $70 million. More importantly, it’s the only company to have published papers describing a machine that achieves economic fusion by design. The approach relies on new technologies that still need to be verified with experiments. Volpe and his team are working to demonstrate them, and this article reviews their recent accomplishments.

Moving heat outward

The plasma inside a fusion machine radiates heat outward, causing the internal surface of the chamber to heat up. This heat must be moved outward to generate steam, press on fan turbine blades, and create electrical power. The easiest way to do this is to pump molten metal toward the hot plasma and then outward. There are two ways to do this: One involves having liquid metal flow in front of the internal surface plate, and the other involves flowing liquid behind the plate.

Liquid metal wall

Flowing liquid in front is referred to as “liquid metal wall.” This is illustrated here, with the liquid metal shown in red.

To get this to work, magnets would need to push the molten metal outward, toward the metal plate, while it flows and removes heat. The alternative cooling method is to expose an approximately 2-mm-thick metal plate to plasma radiation and cool it by flowing molten metal along its back surface within channels.

However, radiation from the plasma will eventually damage the metal plate, requiring replacement. This involves machine disassembly, which is expensive. Therefore, it’s more cost-effective to protect the metal plate with flowing molten metal and rarely disassemble, if at all. In other words, a liquid metal wall is probably required to achieve economic fusion. This is new technology, which means it needs to be verified with experiments.

Volpe’s liquid metal status

Volpe and his team have developed a physical experiment that circulates liquid metal through a cylindrical chamber. Superconducting magnets and currents push the liquid outward so that it coats the internal surface of the chamber wall as it falls from the top to the bottom. They are currently circulating liquid tin at °C and hope to soon circulate liquid lithium at 850°C, which is needed by their design.

Next-generation magnets

Renaissance Fusion’s machine uses irregular-shaped magnets to compress the plasma. In theory, these could be manufactured using 3D printing on a rotating tube via ion deposition. Within a vacuum environment, alternating layers of superconductor and “insulator” could potentially be printed. The illustration below shows how approximately five irregularly-shaped magnets could be printed per tube, within a 12-tube system, producing a total of 60 magnets.

Volpe’s team has created a machine, pictured below, that prints superconductors on a 24-cm-wide tape. This is 20× wider than that typically done in today’s superconducting magnets. This is an intermediate step to printing directly onto a tube.

Renaissance Fusion is the only company developing a fusion machine that produces competitively priced electricity by design, and this makes it one of the most important players in the fusion industry.

data.title: Jorjin Pushes AR Smart Glasses Forward
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

By the time augmented reality (AR) glasses finally move beyond pilots and proofs of concept, much of the heavy lifting may already have been done quietly by companies such as Jorjin Technologies.

Founded in 1997, Taiwan-based Jorjin Technologies built its reputation not as a consumer brand but as an engineering-centric supplier of system-in-packages, system-on-modules (SoMs), and wireless connectivity modules for Tier 1 customers. In its early years, the company worked closely with major IC vendors, and between 2005 and 2007, it supplied communications modules used in Amazon’s first-generation Kindle and Kindle Fire devices.

That deep background in compact, low-power wireless design later became a key enabler for a new direction. In 2013, Jorjin began developing AR smart glasses, initially as an OEM and ODM partner to global brands. Over more than a decade, the company accumulated hands-on experience across industrial, medical, and defense-grade AR systems, well before the technology attracted today’s renewed investor and ecosystem attention.

“We’ve been doing AR glasses for more than 12 years,” Vince Hsu, senior sales director at Jorjin Technologies, said. “We know the advantages and limitations of different light engines, near-eye displays, and human-machine interfaces because we’ve had to design them for very different use cases.”

Most early AR glasses were purpose-built: ruggedized headsets for factory workers, specialized optics for surgeons, or training tools for defense and aerospace. Jorjin’s portfolio reflects that diversity. One U.S.-based startup, for example, used Jorjin-designed AR glasses to support FDA-regulated surgical applications. According to Hsu, more than 3,000 surgical cases ranging from knee to shoulder procedures have used AR glasses built on Jorjin hardware.

In 2020, the company began promoting its own branded AR platform, the J7EF, aimed at more general-purpose applications rather than single verticals. The move reflected growing demand from customers who wanted adaptable hardware that could be paired with their own software and content stacks.

Despite the industry buzz around consumer smart glasses, Jorjin remains firmly B2B-focused for now. “Consumer AR is still very challenging,” Hsu said. “The devices are expensive, relatively heavy, and content is still lacking. That’s why today, our AR business is mainly industrial, medical, and military.”

Still, the company is preparing for the next phase. Jorjin recently developed lightweight engineering samples of AR glasses weighing under 50 grams, with mass production planned as early as this year. While competitors such as Meta and Ray-Ban have demonstrated market appetite for AI-enabled smart glasses without displays, Jorjin is betting on the convergence of AR and AI.

“Our core competency is near-eye display,” Hsu said. “We believe AI glasses without visuals are only a first step. AR with AI is where real value will emerge.”

Wireless modules as a strategic advantage

One differentiator for Jorjin is that its AR ambitions are built on decades of wireless module expertise. Allen Lin, special assistant at Jorjin’s GMO office, described how the company approaches module design as a manufacturing discipline rather than a simple component exercise.

“When you’re a module supplier, you don’t just say ‘it passed,’” Lin said. “Every unit has to be tested, logged, and guaranteed. Customers expect 100% performance because they are integrating you directly into their system.”

Over time, Jorjin moved beyond standalone connectivity modules toward SoMs that integrate application processors, memory, and wireless functions. This approach proved particularly well-suited to smart glasses, where size, power consumption, and thermal constraints are far tighter than in laptops or smartphones.

“Smart glasses forced us to think differently,” Lin said. “Small size, ultra-low power, and mobile use are not optional; they’re fundamental.”

As wireless standards evolve, from Wi-Fi 6 and Bluetooth Low Energy to ultra-wideband, Jorjin is repositioning its module strategy. Rather than offering generic wireless blocks, the company is developing integrated connectivity and sensor modules tailored specifically for wearable platforms.

The idea is to combine wireless MCUs, sensors, audio DSPs, and free processing resources into tightly packaged subsystems that customers can build AI algorithms on top of. Smart glasses are the first target platform.

“In AI glasses, audio is just as important as vision,” Lin said. “Microphones, speakers, DSPs for voice detection—all of these need to work together with connectivity and sensing.”

This modular approach also reflects practical constraints in eyewear design. Battery capacity and connectivity remain the two hardest problems, especially as chipmakers push toward smaller geometries to reduce power consumption. Jorjin is already engaging with IC vendors to explore slimmer packaging and more advanced process nodes that can better support wearable form factors.

Content and spatial computing

While Jorjin started as a hardware supplier, customer demand has pulled it higher up the stack. Many enterprise users already had AR glasses but lacked tools to develop content or manage applications. In response, Jorjin built an in-house content team and later developed an authoring platform that allows customers to create their own AR experiences.

More recently, the company introduced what it calls “MetaSpace,” an immersive environment that combines projection-based spatial computing with AR glasses. The system supports multilayer visualization, inside-out and outside-in tracking, and even millimeter-wave radar sensors that can act as human-machine interfaces.

These solutions are now being deployed in education, exhibition halls, and corporate showrooms, as well as for remote collaboration and digital-twin-style visualization.

For Hsu, the case for AI-driven AR is becoming increasingly clear: “AI needs to know what you are looking at. A watch can’t do that. A pin on your shirt can’t reliably do that. AR glasses can.”

In industrial settings, Jorjin sees AI-enhanced AR enabling hands-free access to SOPs, maintenance manuals, and real-time guidance through large language models. Human experts remain part of the loop, but AI can handle first-level assistance, reducing downtime and training costs. Similar opportunities exist in healthcare and education, where contextual awareness is critical.

Taiwan’s role in the AR ecosystem

Looking ahead, Jorjin expects lightweight AR glasses to gain traction within two to three years as semiconductor processes mature and costs decline. Rather than replacing smartphones, AR glasses are likely to coexist with them, relying on companion devices for compute and connectivity.

Taiwan, Hsu argued, is well-positioned to support this transition. “We have semiconductors, communications, and strong manufacturing engineering,” he said. “It’s also a good place to do proof-of-concept deployments before scaling to Southeast Asia, India, or elsewhere.”

Jorjin is taking advantage of this and is leveraging its wireless and packaging expertise, deep AR experience, and growing AI capabilities to help shape what immersive computing will eventually become.

data.title: Synopsys Brings Agentic Engineering Into Focus as AI-Driven Design Tests Productivity Limits
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

A recent rise in attention around Synopsys, following an activist stake by Elliott Investment Management, is bringing new focus on how AI is evolving from a technical upgrade in engineering workflows into a driver of growth.

From chip design to system-level simulation, AI permeates every layer, promising to compress development cycles from years to months. Faster design cycles and higher iteration counts are no longer just engineering benchmarks; they are increasingly being viewed as indicators of future growth. However, as companies scale compute spending and experiment with generative design and AI-driven simulation, where are the tangible productivity gains emerging, and more importantly, is there a gap between demonstrated capability and repeatable, real-world productivity gains?

EE Times spoke to Prith Banerjee, senior VP of Innovation and a member of the executive leadership team at Synopsys, as the company expands its chip design tools into system-level simulation following its $35 billion acquisition of simulation behemoth Ansys. Banerjee, who previously served as the CTO of Ansys, said Synopsys is betting that AI-driven simulation, generative design, and what it calls “agentic engineering” can significantly reduce design cycles and improve product quality.

Where AI is delivering productivity

The most immediate gains from AI are emerging in simulation and design iteration, areas that have historically been associated with slower engineering cycles.

“A car designer trying to reduce aerodynamic drag would traditionally spend about 100 hours on CAD design and 10,000 hours on simulation,” Banerjee said. “With AI tools such as SimAI and GeoAI, these tasks can be completed in minutes.”

The shift is not just about speed, but scale. “Instead of evaluating two or three designs, engineers can evaluate thousands,” he said.

In a recent company statement, Synopsys said its agentic design workflows are already showing measurable gains, with productivity improvements of 2× and, in some cases, up to 5× for complex chip design tasks that traditionally take four to six months.

This is where the return on investment is becoming visible. Faster simulation reduces cycle time, while generative approaches expand the design space that engineers can realistically explore. In practice, this means teams can move from a handful of design iterations to hundreds or thousands within the same development window.

Synopsys is pushing toward a more autonomous model of engineering, otherwise referred to as agentic engineering. “We are moving from assistive AI to more autonomous systems,” Banerjee said. “Our vision is what we call agentic engineering.”

In this model, AI agents take on larger parts of the workflow, using design and simulation tools on behalf of engineers. Last week, EE Times reported that “Synopsys announced its first L4 agentic workflow at Converge. This workflow can, for example, handle architectural spec to RTL, build test plans, and handle formal verification, static verification, coverage, and debug with separate task agents.”

The idea is to move beyond tools that assist individual steps, toward systems that can execute multi-step processes with limited human intervention. In a separate conversation with EE Times, Satya Nitta, co-founder and CEO of agentic AI startup Emergence AI, explained that many of these systems still operate in iterative loops rather than as fully autonomous decision-makers. “If one model is correct 95% of the time and another is also correct 95%, the combined system cannot reach 100% accuracy,” Nitta said. “Agentic systems work in loops… a verifier checks them… if not confident, it asks the system to try again.”

This suggests that autonomy in engineering is evolving as a layered process, where generation and verification remain tightly coupled rather than fully independent. As workflows become more automated, issues around verification, traceability, and trust become more complex, particularly in regulated industries. For now, most engineering teams remain in the assistive phase, where AI supports decision-making rather than executing autonomously.

Digital twins: useful, but not fully trusted

Digital twins are often positioned as a way to reduce reliance on physical testing and verification, but their role within production workflows is still evolving. “The goal is to reach very high accuracy, but it will never be 100%,” Banerjee said. “Today, we may be at around 90% accuracy, and we are working toward 95% and 99%.”

The company is also pushing digital twin platforms that connect chip design, software behavior, and full-system validation. In automotive use cases, Synopsys said such platforms can enable up to 90% of software validation before hardware is available. At that level, digital models are useful for exploring scenarios and narrowing down design options. “This allows you to run different scenarios without testing them physically,” he said.

But physical validation has not gone away. Banerjee noted that “about 75% of [R&D] goes into physical testing and prototyping,” reflecting how much of engineering still depends on real-world verification.

The result is a hybrid model. Digital twins are increasingly used to reduce the number of physical iterations, but not to eliminate them entirely, particularly in safety-critical domains such as automotive and aerospace.

For engineers, the distinction matters. Digital twins are effective for prediction and iteration, but final validation still depends on physical systems, regulatory requirements, and safety standards.

That limitation is not unique to simulation models. Across the industry, engineers are also grappling with the probabilistic nature of AI systems themselves. Nitta said that even high-performing models can fall short in critical environments. “In many situations, 99% accuracy is still not acceptable,” he said. “You cannot rely only on probabilistic systems… you need deterministic systems that enforce rules.”

This has practical implications for how digital twins and AI-driven simulation are deployed. While models can narrow down design choices, final decisions, especially in regulated industries, still require deterministic validation and consistency across runs.

AI compute and infrastructure

The productivity gains from AI come with a different kind of cost. “AI does require significant compute resources,” Banerjee said. “Some models have trillions of parameters… which require substantial computational power.”

Training and deploying AI models for simulation is not the same as running traditional engineering workloads. It introduces a dependency on high-performance computing infrastructure, particularly GPUs, and shifts costs toward data processing and model training.

For companies scaling these approaches, the tradeoff is not just between tools and talent, but between software efficiency and infrastructure investment. In practical terms, budgets that were once allocated to licenses and engineering hours are increasingly being shared with compute infrastructure.

Synopsys aligns closely with compute ecosystem players, including collaborations with AMD and Microsoft to run EDA tools on cloud platforms, highlighting how engineering workflows become tightly coupled with large-scale compute infrastructure.

Other industry approaches include attempts to reduce this HPC infrastructure dependency by shifting toward smaller, task-specific models during deployment, particularly in inference-heavy enterprise environments.

What AI is not replacing

Despite the push toward automation, Banerjee drew a clear boundary. “AI is not replacing engineering judgement,” he said. “Engineers will become more capable, but the responsibility remains with them.”

The comparison, he noted, is similar to the evolution of programming, from low-level coding to higher-level abstractions and now code generation tools. The role of the engineer changes, but accountability does not.

This becomes important in areas such as safety, certification, and liability. Regardless of how much of the workflow is automated, responsibility for design decisions and system performance still rests with human engineers and the organizations behind them.

While much of the current momentum is tied to applications and digital infrastructure, moving deeper into system-level engineering will require capabilities that go beyond software, including hardware design, compute infrastructure, and product development at scale.

AI is already reducing design time, the number of iterations, and improving product quality. However, the gains are uneven, depend heavily on compute, and are still evolving across industries. What is changing is the level of expectation. As companies such as Synopsys face increasing pressure to translate AI capabilities into sustained growth, the link between engineering productivity and business performance is becoming more direct.

Engineering teams now have to decide not just whether to adopt AI, but where it delivers measurable value, and where it still depends on assumptions that have yet to play out at scale.
